video_title: Louis-Vincent Gave: How China's AI Strategy Kills U.S. Tech Margins
gist: Louis-Vincent Gave (Gavekal) argues China's open-source AI and semiconductor push will commoditize U.S. LLMs and chips whose valuations assume they never will, while a structural bond bear market, weaponized rule of law, and loss of U.S. control over sea lanes push investors toward commodities, cyclicals, financials, and away from crowded Big Tech. Discussion also covers oil trapped in a $65-100 China-set range with blown-out crack spreads, dollar weakness masked by a euro-heavy DXY, and earnings quality inflated by unrealized investment gains.

themes.theme: China's open-source AI vs U.S. fortress model

insights.claim: OpenAI and Anthropic are pursuing a closed 'fortress' strategy (high walls, come inside to be safe) while China is building an open 'Dubai' where anyone enters and leaves freely and modifies the model, an ironic role reversal given each country's history.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: The open vs closed architecture split, not just price, is the axis on which the AI platform war is fought; open-source distribution could win global developer share.
insights.quote: Open AAI and Tropic are essentially looking to build medieval fortresses with huge walls and say come into our fortress and you'll be safe here to the Goldman Sachs of this world and the JP Morgans etc. And China's building Dubai.

insights.claim: China was forced into open-sourcing its AI models specifically because U.S. chip export controls denied it the compute to pursue a closed, capital-intensive approach, turning a constraint into a distribution advantage that is 'anathema' to China's usual throw-money-at-it approach.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: U.S. export controls may have backfired by pushing China toward an open-source strategy that erodes U.S. tech pricing power globally.
insights.quote: This time, the US told them, "You can't do it because we're not going to sell you the chips." So, they had to find workarounds. The only workaround was to open source it

insights.claim: Chinese AI models offer a 'Toyota/BYD' good-enough product versus the U.S. 'Ferrari,' and both U.S. LLMs and semiconductors are currently priced to never be commoditized even though China's entry historically commoditizes any space it enters.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: If commoditization arrives, the AI/semiconductor complex is mispriced; watch for LLM and chip pricing power to break.
insights.quote: right now both of these are priced to essentially never be commoditized um when we know that when China enters a space it gets commoditized

insights.claim: China's AI business model is a bait-and-switch: give away a 'Ferrari for $10,000' (near-free access) to win users, then raise the price to its true value later, but the shift from token-maxing to token-minimizing already suggests enterprises may not play along.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: The durability of AI monetization is unproven; a customer refusal to pay up would undercut the entire revenue narrative funding the buildout.
insights.quote: The business model is for them to turn around at some point and say, by the way, it's no longer $10,000. It's actually $300,000.

insights.claim: Three-quarters of the startups that Marc Andreessen's VC firm sees now use Chinese LLMs because they are essentially free, illustrating global adoption of Chinese open-source models well beyond price-sensitive markets.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Global developer capture by Chinese models threatens the installed-base moat U.S. AI valuations rely on.
insights.quote: threearters of the startups they see use Chinese LLMs because they're essentially free

insights.claim: Microsoft is reportedly considering adding DeepSeek's model to its offering after previously routing between OpenAI and Anthropic, signaling that even the U.S. hyperscaler most tied to OpenAI is hedging toward Chinese open-source.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Hyperscaler willingness to integrate Chinese models validates commoditization risk and pressures U.S. model providers' pricing.

insights.claim: Chinese memory makers CXMT and YMTC (Yangtze Memory) are coming after Micron's ~85% gross margins, and China's tendency to prioritize market share over profit is a direct threat to record U.S. tech margins.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Micron's DRAM margins are a specific short-thesis target; investors should track Chinese memory capacity ramps.
insights.quote: that 85% gross profit margin that Micron has today, they're coming after it.

insights.claim: Semiconductors now make up roughly a fifth of the global equity index (up from ~2% of the global benchmark historically), despite being the most capital-intensive and cyclical industry, creating systemic index risk when sales growth rolls over.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Passive and benchmarked investors are structurally over-exposed to the most cyclical sector at peak concentration.
insights.quote: Semiconductors are now essentially a fifth of the global equity index

insights.claim: In the second quarter, global market cap rose $10 trillion, of which $7 trillion was semiconductors, driven by a 'not enough chips for everyone' scarcity narrative that mirrors Q1's energy scarcity trade.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Scarcity leadership rotates quarter to quarter; identifying the next scarcity theme (Gave suggests refining) is the trade.
insights.quote: the global market cap increased by 10 trillion, seven of which was semiconductors

insights.claim: TSMC, SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics now make up ~40% of the Asia MSCI benchmark, forcing Asian managers capped at 10% per stock to buy 'crappier' proxy semiconductor names they don't want, which will have no market when the cycle turns.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Forced ownership of second-tier semis creates a liquidity air pocket on the downside; avoid the proxy names.
insights.quote: you now have three companies, TSMC, SK Highness, and Samsung Electronics that make up 40% of your benchmark uh for the Asia Msei

insights.claim: Taiwan's exports rose 50% and Korea is booming (Samsung workers getting half-million-dollar bonuses) on the semiconductor cycle, so a semiconductor rollover would devastate not just the stock market but the Korean and Taiwanese economies.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: The chip cycle is now a macro tail risk for Korea/Taiwan equities and currencies, not just individual stocks.
insights.quote: Samsung workers are getting half a million dollar bonuses.

themes.theme: Oil range, crack spreads and refiners

insights.claim: China effectively sets the floor and ceiling on oil, buying aggressively at $65 and below into massive storage (officially ~1.3 billion barrels, likely closer to 1.8 billion) and stepping away above $100, trapping crude in a roughly $65-100 range.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: A China-managed $65-100 band is a tradeable regime for energy names and volatility positioning.
insights.quote: at 65, they buy as much as they can. 65 and below and at 100 they stop buying.

insights.claim: A stable $65-100 oil range is bullish for energy company cash flows because it discourages new capex and capacity growth while keeping cash flows healthy, a scenario CEOs would 'sign with both hands.'
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Range-bound oil favors capital discipline and shareholder returns over growth-driven energy names.
insights.quote: 65 to 100 I'll sign with both hands. It's like it's a happy days because there's not going to be great capex.

insights.claim: Crack spreads have blown out (~$60 in the US) and could exceed the oil price itself for the first time ever, driven by Russia/Ukraine bombing each other's refineries, six Gulf refineries knocked out in the Iran war, and China hoarding refined products.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Refining margins, not crude, are where the energy edge is; refiners benefit structurally from 20 years of no new capacity.
insights.quote: it feels like we're going to get to the point where spreads are crack spreads are higher than the oil price which is of of course never happened.

insights.claim: U.S. refiners like Valero and Marathon Petroleum are at all-time highs and breaking out, generating enormous free cash yields they will return via buybacks (Marathon buying ~5-10% of its stock annually) rather than building new refineries.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Refiners are a high-buyback, high-free-cash-flow trade that Gave calls a 'fairly obvious' way to play blown-out crack spreads.
insights.quote: Marathon is going to be buying five or 10% of their stock uh like for the next few years

insights.claim: Russia is halting refined-product exports through at least end of July, and since Russia provides ~11% of the world's diesel, diesel prices jumped this week, raising the value of Chinese 'teapot' refiners that could replace Russian supply.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Diesel supply loss is a near-term inflationary catalyst and a bid for Chinese refining capacity.
insights.quote: Russia provides about 11% of the world's diesel

themes.theme: Loss of U.S. sea-lane control and stockpiling commodities

insights.claim: The biggest lesson of the Iran war is that the U.S. no longer controls the world's sea lanes because you cannot defend billion-dollar ships with million-dollar missiles against $10,000 drones, ending 80 years of assumed U.S. Navy protection of global trade.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: A world without guaranteed sea-lane security raises structural risk premia for trade-dependent assets and justifies naval buildouts.
insights.quote: we now live in a world where you cannot protect billion dollar ships with million-dollar missiles against $10,000 drones. The maths no longer add up.

insights.claim: The key lesson for allocators from the Strait of Hormuz crisis is that stockpiling physical commodities (fertilizer, oil, rare earths, pharmaceuticals) now beats holding treasuries or even gold, because in a fractured world you cannot assume you can buy critical inputs when needed.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Gave says he remains a gold bull but is 'much more bullish' on other commodities; strategic-reserve building is a multi-year commodity bid.
insights.quote: rather than treasuries, rather than gold, you actually need to stockpile commodities.

insights.claim: Governments are already moving to strategic stockpiling: the U.S. DoD will start stockpiling lithium and Germany plans a strategic natural-gas reserve, and building this storage itself consumes steel, plastics and commodities, creating double demand.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Strategic reserve construction is a durable, multi-year underlying bid across commodities and industrial materials.
insights.quote: the department of defense in the US it's going to start stockpiling lithium

insights.claim: China refusing to sell India fertilizer during the crisis (India relies on the Gulf for ~40% of fertilizer) is teaching import-dependent nations that friends may not deliver, likely reversing protectionist bans on Chinese cars and solar panels.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Supply-security fears could expand Chinese export markets for EVs and solar despite prior bans, reshaping trade flows.

themes.theme: AI capex, funding strain and earnings quality

insights.claim: The hyperscalers enjoyed an effectively zero cost of capital after years of great returns, letting them abandon their usual ROIC discipline and throw 20x more money at AI than at cloud; now that cost of capital is rising as rates climb.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Rising cost of capital removes the free-money underpinning of the AI capex boom and pressures return hurdles.
insights.quote: these guys had an essentially an free cost of capital, they could afford for once to be quite, you know, free with it.

insights.claim: Amazon's 2026 capex guidance jumped from an initial $100-120 billion to $180 billion, its debt rose ~$100 billion with another $25 billion being sold, illustrating hyperscalers leaning heavily on balance sheets to fund AI.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Debt-funded capex escalation raises credit risk and questions the sustainability of the buildout if returns disappoint.
insights.quote: Amazon's capex when they came into the year, I think they had guided to 100 to 120 and then last quarter, I think they guided to 180.

insights.claim: OpenAI is deferring its IPO to 2027 even though it needs equity capital now, and it is strategically 'too big to fail' in the sense that if it fails it brings down the entire AI ecosystem, not because government would bail it out.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: OpenAI's funding path is a systemic linchpin for the AI complex; delay signals funding stress worth monitoring.
insights.quote: if open AI fails it brings down everything.

insights.claim: AI capex has become so large it drove roughly three-quarters of the improvement in U.S. GDP in recent quarters, with McKinsey projecting $6.5 trillion of spend between now and 2030 and ~$700 billion this year.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: The macro economy is now dangerously dependent on AI capex; a pullback would directly hit GDP.
insights.quote: it was threequarters of the improvement in US GDP and the numbers are now so big

insights.claim: The market wrongly assumes AI is a three-year capex cycle followed by pure profit harvest, but embedded maintenance capex and depreciation are huge, with Oracle spending ~100% of revenue on capex and data-center leases (some guaranteed 30 years) functioning as hidden debt.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: The 'year four rainbow' of AI profitability is a mirage; ongoing chip-upgrade capex and lease obligations will suppress free cash flow.
insights.quote: there's no rainbow in year four here.

insights.claim: The fastest-growing part of U.S. corporate earnings is 'other income' from unrealized gains on investments (e.g. booking an Anthropic stake marked from $100 billion to $1 trillion), meaning headline earnings strength is largely financial engineering.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Reported earnings beats overstate operating strength; strip out investment gains and semis to see true profitability.
insights.quote: what part of the earnings has been growing the fastest the other income which is all the investments

insights.claim: Q2 earnings were up ~28%, but excluding other income it was ~17%, and excluding semiconductors it was mid-single digits, so the 'off the charts' earnings narrative collapses under the hood.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: Ex-semis, ex-investment-gains earnings growth is unremarkable, undercutting the bullish earnings case for the broad index.
insights.quote: If you look at Q2 earnings that were up like 28%. If you take out the other income, it was like 17%. If you take out semiconductors, it was like mid-s single digits.

insights.claim: The divergence between S&P 500 earnings and national-income earnings (tax-receipt-based) is at a record stretch matching 2000 and 2007 tops, historically a sign of peak financial engineering and market extremes.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Use national-income earnings as a cleaner signal; extreme divergence has previously marked cyclical peaks.
insights.quote: today you are back at one of these stretches just like you had in 2000, just like you had in 2007 of record divergence between S&P 500 earnings and national income earnings

themes.theme: Structural bond bear market and foreign ownership risk

insights.claim: We are in a structural bond bear market that began ~five years ago with COVID policy failures, with sticky above-comfort inflation across the US, Japan, France and UK making bonds vulnerable to any oil shock tipping into inflationary bust.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Duration remains structurally unattractive; the bond bear market is a multi-year regime, not a cyclical dip.
insights.quote: we're we're in a structural bond bear market. uh we have been for five years

insights.claim: The real driver of sovereign debt crises is not debt-to-GDP but the share of debt held by foreigners; France and Britain (~30% foreign-held, adjusting for ECB) are far more vulnerable to a crisis than the US, whose reserve-currency status always finds price-sensitive buyers.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Watch foreign ownership ratios, not headline debt levels; French OATs and UK gilts are the more likely crisis candidates.
insights.quote: The problem is never debt to GDP. The problem is always what percentage of your debt is owned by foreigners

insights.claim: Japanese investors hold ~$3.5 trillion of foreign assets (~80% of Japan's GDP), and as JGB yields rise toward 4.5% and the yen stays cheap, a repatriation home would be a 'Damocles sword' over global bond markets that lack a buyer on the other side.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: A Japanese repatriation is a major tail risk for US, French and other bond markets reliant on Japanese buying.
insights.quote: Japanese investors own three and a half trillion US dollars worth of foreign assets. That's 80% of their GDP

insights.claim: Central banks will keep short rates artificially low because governments now fund at the short end (a third of US debt rolls over in the next 18 months), so investment in short-term funding has locked them into low rates while the long end sells off, steepening yield curves globally.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: A structurally steepening yield curve favors banks/financials and argues against long-duration bonds.
insights.quote: A third of US government debt rolls over the next 18 months. Worsh can't raise interest rates when you got a third of the the debt that that that rolls over.

insights.claim: Japan has been the trailblazer for global monetary policy (first to ZIRP, QE, and negative rates, each later copied), and now with 3% inflation but rates at 1% it is deliberately dragging its feet, letting the long end sell off and bank stocks rise daily.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Japan's steep curve is a template; Japanese and global banks benefit from central banks tolerating higher inflation.
insights.quote: Japan has been the trailblazer on monetary policy for everybody else.

insights.claim: The DXY is a misleading 'index of the world of 25 years ago' (about two-thirds euro, a quarter yen, no China), so the dollar's genuine weakness against emerging-market and commodity currencies is masked even when the euro-heavy index looks stable.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Investors should track the dollar against a basket of EM and commodity crosses, not the DXY, to read true direction.
insights.quote: It's the index of a world of 25 years ago when Japan was really important and Europe was really important. It's not the index of the next 10 20 years.

insights.claim: China has clearly shifted to a stronger-renminbi policy, with the RMB rising ~50 basis points per month like clockwork for 18 months, while managing other Asian currencies (Singapore dollar, Thai baht, ringgit, peso) against the renminbi rather than the dollar.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: A deliberate RMB appreciation reshapes Asian FX; the rubber band between a weak yen and strong RMB is set to snap.
insights.quote: if you look at the past 18 months, the REMBI is going up 50 basis point a month like clockwork.

insights.claim: China now settles more than half of its trade in its own currency and its CIPS payment system has risen from ~3% to ~8% share, making de-dollarization 'not an event but a process' that continues for years as China gains monetary independence.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Gradual RMB trade settlement erodes dollar dominance structurally; a slow-burn negative for long-term dollar demand.
insights.quote: China now settles more than half of its trade in its own currency.

insights.claim: The MAG7 stocks have become a global reserve asset that every ex-US fund and sovereign wealth fund (ADIA, Norway, Swiss National Bank buying the top-20 market caps) is forced to own, so if the AI trade rolls over the dollar resumes its downtrend as this global crowding unwinds.
insights.speaker: Peter Boockvar
insights.so_what: The dollar and the AI trade are linked; a Big Tech rollover is a specific bearish catalyst for the dollar.
insights.quote: Hyperscaler trade, MAG7 trade, AI trade rolls over. Let's just assume. Does the dollar resume its downward trend?

insights.claim: China effectively operates two currencies and two central banks (the renminbi/PBOC and the Hong Kong dollar/HKMA), so the Hong Kong dollar peg will last 'forever' as an all-upside 'tokenized US dollar' China can offer counterparties unwilling to trade in renminbi.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: The HKD peg is durable; China's dual-currency structure gives it unique flexibility in trade settlement.
insights.quote: China is kind of unique uh in the history of countries in that it's the first sort of financial power because it is becoming a financial power to have two currencies and two central banks.

insights.claim: The 2022 seizure of Russian state and oligarch assets without due process undermined the West's biggest comparative advantage (the rule of law) and triggered the current gold bull market as central banks flipped from 25-year net sellers to massive net buyers.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Sanctions-driven distrust is a structural, durable bid for gold from central banks and non-Western individuals.
insights.quote: the lesson of 2022 was don't buy treasuries, buy gold. Don't buy OECD bonds.

insights.claim: Gold ran from ~$2,000 two years ago to as high as $5,500 and has pulled back to ~$4,200, with a sharp air pocket during the war when Dubai (roughly a quarter of the world's physical gold market and India's buying hub) shut down amid drone attacks.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave / Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Physical market chokepoints (Dubai) can cause sharp gold dislocations; context for the recent pullback from highs.
insights.quote: Dubai is roughly a quarter of the physical gold market in the world and essentially that market shut down

themes.theme: Macro regime and positioning: cyclicals and financials

insights.claim: Gave is overweight cyclicals and financials over tech, judging by fundamentals, momentum, positioning and valuation: tech has stretched valuations, extreme positioning and stalling momentum, while financials have exciting fundamentals, underweight positioning, building momentum and still-attractive valuations.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: A concrete rotation trade: overweight financials and cyclicals, underweight crowded Big Tech.
insights.quote: in terms of the markets, I'd be overweight cyclicals and financials.

insights.claim: Financials are breaking out globally (US, European banks, historically distressed Chinese and Japanese banks) as the world moves to a high demand for capital and steepening yield curves, a broad-based bank bull market across regions.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Bank outperformance is a global, multi-driver theme worth owning across US, Europe, Japan and China.
insights.quote: European banks are breaking out. Chinese banks, everybody's like, you know, everybody's favorite dog with fleas

insights.claim: The market repriced from two Fed rate cuts to two rate hikes, but Gave and Boockvar both expect neither hike to materialize over the next six months, implying the long end sells off and the dollar is not as firm as consensus assumes.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: Positioning for a dovish surprise (no hikes) argues for a weaker dollar, steeper curve, and cyclical/commodity exposure.
insights.quote: Do you think that over the next six months we get those two rate hikes? I personally don't.

insights.claim: We are in an inflationary boom with high inflation and fine economic activity because every government runs budget deficits of 4-7% of GDP (US at 7% with full employment), and in such regimes investors chase scarcity, rotating from energy to semiconductors to potentially refining.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: In an inflationary boom, own the next scarcity theme; deficits sustain nominal growth and inflation.
insights.quote: we're in an inflationary boom environment. In inflationary boom environments, investors look for scarcity.

insights.claim: China's guiding priority is domestic social stability, not geopolitical mischief, so it stabilized rather than weaponized the oil market (declining to spike oil to $150 to hurt Trump's midterms) because it prefers a humming US consumer to buy from over a 20-year horizon.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: China's behavior is predictable through a domestic-stability lens, not an axis-of-chaos narrative; it is a stabilizing force on oil.
insights.quote: first and foremost China cares about domestic political stability and $150 oil would would be bad would be bad for China.

insights.claim: Xi Jinping is fundamentally an imperialist whose Belt and Road, Silk Road Fund and Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank are classic imperial road-building projects to import commodities cheaply, transform them in China, and sell finished goods (cars, tractors, telecom) back, with military buildup the later 'friction' phase to protect trade.
insights.speaker: Louis-Vincent Gave
insights.so_what: China's naval and military expansion follows an empire's logic of protecting trade routes, not territorial conquest, reframing the China military-threat narrative.
insights.quote: the history of every empire is fundamentally a road building exercise.
